title: Audio Descriptions and Interactive Media
learningObjectives: Explain what audio descriptions are and determine whether multimedia content in your courses needs them
Test interactive media (simulations, H5P, embeds) for keyboard and screen reader accessibility
Identify when to handle accessibility issues yourself versus escalating to your institution's accessibility office

keyTerms.definition: Narration that describes the visual elements of a video — what happens on screen, how objects move, what people look like, changes in scene or lighting. Audio description is inserted during natural pauses in dialogue so it does not interrupt the main soundtrack. It is essential for students who are blind or have low vision.

A version of a video where the main dialogue and action are paused to allow time for more detailed audio description of complex visual information. Extended AD is used when a standard AD is insufficient because there are not enough natural pauses. Sometimes called "descriptive video service" (DVS) on commercial media.

Multimedia elements that users can interact with — not just watch or listen. Examples: H5P activities, PhET simulations, Labster virtual labs, Adobe Captivate interactions, and learning games. These must be keyboard-accessible and work with screen readers.

A situation where keyboard users cannot escape from an interactive element using standard keyboard navigation (Tab, Escape, arrow keys). Examples: a modal dialog with no close button, an embedded game where Tab cycles infinitely within the game.

An accessible text-based or alternative version of multimedia content. For a science demo video, the alternative might be a detailed written protocol. For an interactive simulation, the alternative might be a worksheet or exam question that tests the same learning objective without requiring the interactive experience.

contentBlocks.title: Audio Descriptions: What Faculty Need to Know
contentBlocks.body:
Audio description is probably the most misunderstood accessibility requirement for video. Faculty often ask: "Do all my videos need audio descriptions?" The answer is: **probably not, but some do**. Understanding when AD is required versus recommended is important.

**What Audio Description Is (And Is Not)**

Audio description is **not** a complete transcript read aloud. It is **narration that describes visual-only information** — what happens on screen that is not conveyed through dialogue.

Example: A biology demonstration video shows a teacher mixing two chemical solutions. As they pour, the liquids change color and fizz.

- **Dialogue in the video**: "Notice how the color changes. This indicates a chemical reaction."
- **What AD adds** (during the pause after "reaction"): "The clear solution turns deep blue as the red liquid is added. Bubbles rise from the beaker."

Without AD, a blind student hears the teacher's explanation but misses the visual evidence supporting it. AD fills that gap.

**When AD Is Required**

Under WCAG and ADA Title II, **pre-recorded video with visual information necessary to understand the content** requires audio description:

- A chemistry lab demo where the color change is evidence of the reaction
- A physics lecture showing an experiment where motion or visual process is central to understanding
- An art history lecture describing brushwork, composition, or color
- A dance or movement demonstration
- A surgery or medical procedure filmed
- Any video where "you had to see it to understand it"

The key test: **If all the important information is conveyed through dialogue and captions, AD is not required.** If there is important visual-only information, AD is required.

**When AD Is Not Required (Or Less Critical)**

- A talking-head lecture where the instructor is the only visual element (AD is nice to have but not required)
- A slide-based presentation where slides are described verbally in real-time (you are already providing audio description)
- A video with captions and good dialogue that covers all content
- Published films or documentary content where the filmmakers' artistic intent depends on the visual presentation (though high-quality DVD releases often include AD)

**How to Provide AD**

There are a few options:

1. **Video with a separate AD track**: A second audio channel plays alongside the main audio. Users can select which audio track to listen to. This is professional-quality but requires technical editing.

2. **Embedded AD (video with AD mixed in)**: The audio description is inserted into the main soundtrack during natural pauses. This is simpler than a separate track but less flexible for users.

3. **Extended AD version**: A separate version of the video is created with pauses to allow longer descriptions. Useful when there are not enough natural pauses.

4. **Text-based alternative**: Instead of audio description, provide a detailed transcript or description document that walks through what happens visually in the video. A student can read this while watching, or it can be used standalone.

**The Practical Reality for Faculty**

Fully accessible AD is time-consuming. For a 20-minute lecture video, adding audio description can take 1–2 hours of editing. Many faculty do not have this capacity.

Here is a pragmatic approach:

- **If you record lectures with slides**: Describe the slides verbally as you present them. That **is** audio description. No additional work needed.
- **If you use published videos without AD**: Check whether they already include AD (most modern educational videos do). If not, request it from the publisher or provide a text description document.
- **If you create a demo or visual lecture**: Provide a text-based companion document describing visuals in detail. This satisfies WCAG requirements and is faster than video editing.
- **If AD is essential and resources allow**: Contact your library, media services, or disability services office — many institutions can help create AD or outsource it.

The goal is not perfection — it is **providing equivalent access through some means**. A detailed description document is a valid alternative to embedded audio description.

contentBlocks.title: Does This Video Need Audio Description? A Decision Tree with Real Examples
contentBlocks.body:
Let's work through four scenarios to build intuition:

**Scenario 1: Lecture Capture (Instructor + Slides)**

A history professor records a lecture using lecture-capture software (Panopto, Kaltura, etc.). The recording shows the professor on one side and their PowerPoint slides on the other. They talk through the slides, pointing to images and reading text.

**Analysis**: The professor is verbally describing the slides as they present them. A student with no vision can hear the professor say "As you can see in this map, the trade routes ran north-south through the Appalachian valleys" while the slide is displayed. The professor is providing audio description in real-time.

**AD Required?** No. The visual information (the map) is being described verbally.

**What to do**: Ensure the video has captions (so deaf/hard of hearing students get full content) and the slides are available for download separately (so students can review them offline).

---

**Scenario 2: Chemistry Lab Demo**

A chemistry professor recorded a 10-minute video showing the synthesis of a compound. Viewers see the professor adding solutions, adjusting equipment, and observing color changes and reactions. The professor narrates: "I'm adding the reagent now. Watch the color change — this indicates the reaction is happening."

But the video does not show close-ups of the color change or the reaction vessel. The audio description "the solution turns from clear to bright blue" is not present.

**Analysis**: The visual phenomenon (color change) is **evidence** for the reaction the professor is explaining. A student without vision hears "watch the color change" but cannot actually see it, even though they understand the concept. They are missing essential instructional information.

**AD Required?** Yes. Either provide audio description ("The clear solution in the beaker turns deep blue as the reagent is added") or an equivalent alternative (a detailed text description, a written lab protocol, or an on-campus lab visit where the demo is repeated with a live guide).

---

**Scenario 3: Documentary Film**

A professor assigned a 30-minute documentary about climate change. The film is published and professionally produced. It includes archival footage, animations showing climate processes, interviews with scientists, and scenes from affected communities.

The film's narrative is strong — the voiceover and interviews convey most of the information. But the visual impact — seeing polar ice melting, communities underwater, starving polar bears — is part of how the documentary persuades viewers.

**Analysis**: This is a published work. Professional documentaries are protected by copyright, and filmmakers have artistic control. Additionally, while the visuals are powerful, a student can learn the core content (climate change is happening, it has causes and effects, solutions exist) through the narration and captions. However, the visual experience is diminished.

**AD Required?** Not legally required for compliance (the film was not created by the university). Ethically recommended. The professor could:
- Check whether the film has an AD track (many recent releases do)
- Provide a document describing the main visual sequences
- Show the film to the class and arrange for a live guide for students with vision disabilities

---

**Scenario 4: Screen Recording with Voiceover**

A computer science professor recorded a 15-minute screen recording showing how to debug a Python program. The screen shows code, an IDE, and a console with output. The professor narrates: "Click on the breakpoint here. Step through the code. The variable 'count' should be 5 at this point. Let's check its value in the console."

The professor is describing what they are doing and why, but they are not explicitly saying "I'm clicking on line 23" or "the output shows count equals 5."

**Analysis**: The code and console output are visual, but the professor's narration is actually describing them reasonably well. A student listening can follow along — they just cannot see the exact formatting or syntax. Adding AD like "Line 23 is now highlighted in the debugger" would be helpful but might be overkill. A transcript of the narration plus an accessible copy of the code sample would be sufficient.

**AD Required?** Not strictly, but accessibility can be improved. The professor should:
- Provide captions (for accuracy and for deaf/hard of hearing students)
- Provide a downloadable copy of the code file used in the demo
- Provide a written summary or transcript of key steps

---

**Quick Decision Flow**:

```
Is the video primarily an instructor talking with visual aids?
→ No AD required; ensure captions and slides are available

Does the video show a process, experiment, or visual demonstration that is central to understanding?
→ AD or equivalent text alternative required

Is it published content (film, documentary, commercial video)?
→ Check if AD exists; if not, provide text description

Is the instructor describing visuals as they show them (live demo, lecture capture)?
→ No additional AD needed; ensure captions
```

contentBlocks.title: Interactive Media: Testing for Keyboard and Screen Reader Access
contentBlocks.body:
Many courses now include interactive elements — H5P activities, PhET simulations, Labster labs, Kahoot quizzes, or publisher modules. These are wonderful for engagement, but accessibility is uneven.

**What to Test**

When you embed interactive media in your course, ask:

1. **Can it be operated with keyboard only?** Try using only Tab, Enter, Space, and arrow keys. No mouse allowed.
   - Can you start the activity?
   - Can you navigate to every interactive element (buttons, sliders, dropdowns)?
   - Can you see a visible focus indicator showing which element is selected?
   - Can you exit the activity or return to the main page?

2. **Does it work with a screen reader?** (Test with NVDA or JAWS if possible; many institutions have testing licenses available.)
   - Are buttons, links, and form fields announced?
   - Are their purposes clear? ("Submit Quiz" is clear; "Button" is not.)
   - Are instructions and error messages announced?

3. **Is there a keyboard trap?** Try pressing Tab repeatedly. Do you get stuck cycling within one element, unable to move to the next one?

4. **Can focus be moved away with Escape?** If you enter a modal dialog or dropdown, does Escape close it and return focus?

**Real Examples**

**H5P Activities**: Most H5P activities are keyboard-accessible and work well with screen readers. Test with Tab key navigation. Some gaps exist with certain question types (drag-and-drop, hotspot), but generally H5P is a solid choice.

**PhET Simulations**: PhET has made huge strides in accessibility. Many simulations now include keyboard shortcuts and descriptions for blind users. Check the sim's accessibility documentation before assigning.

**Labster Virtual Labs**: Labster labs vary in accessibility. Some are interactive and keyboard-navigable; others are more visual and require clicking. Contact Labster support to ask about accessibility before adopting.

**Publisher Interactive Modules**: Publisher content is wildly variable. A widget that works beautifully on desktop might be inaccessible on mobile. Interactive simulations might not have keyboard alternatives. Always test before assigning.

**What to Do When Interactive Media Is Not Accessible**

**Option 1: Contact the vendor/publisher.**

Many companies are responsive to accessibility requests, especially if you explain that your institution requires WCAG compliance. A simple email: "We are considering adopting your product in our online course. We need to ensure it is keyboard-accessible and works with screen readers. Do you have an accessibility roadmap or testing report?" Often leads to useful responses.

**Option 2: Provide an alternative activity.**

If the interactive element teaches a learning objective (analyzing data, practicing a skill, exploring a concept), and the interactive version is not accessible, you can create a text-based or alternative activity that meets the same objective.

Example: A physics simulation is not keyboard-accessible. Instead, you provide a dataset from the simulation and ask students to analyze it in a spreadsheet, or answer a written problem set covering the same learning objectives.

**Option 3: Arrange accommodations.**

If the interactive element is important for your course and no alternative exists, work with your Disability Services office. They may provide one-on-one support, modified instructions, or alternative assessments for students with disabilities.

**Option 4: Use keyboard accessible alternatives.**

Before embedding an inaccessible tool, check whether an accessible alternative exists:

- Instead of a drag-and-drop quiz → use a dropdown or checkbox quiz
- Instead of a visual game → use a text-based version or worksheet
- Instead of a proprietary simulation → use PhET, which is free and generally accessible

**Pro Tip**: Before assigning a new interactive tool, do a 5-minute keyboard-only test yourself. Try to complete one activity using only Tab and arrow keys. If you get stuck, your students with motor or vision disabilities will, too.

Not all videos need audio description, but any video with visual-only information essential to learning requires either AD or a text alternative. Use the decision tree to determine if your content needs AD. For interactive media (simulations, H5P, games), test keyboard accessibility by using Tab and arrow keys to navigate. If interactive elements are not accessible, contact the vendor, provide an alternative activity, or work with your Disability Services office. The goal is equivalent access to learning content, whether through the original medium or through an accessible alternative.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Think about multimedia content in your courses — videos, simulations, interactive elements. Pick one example. Evaluate it: (1) Does it have captions (if audio is present)? (2) Does it require audio description based on the decision tree in this section? (3) If it includes interactive elements, try navigating it with keyboard only (Tab, Enter, Space — no mouse). What worked, and where did you get stuck? Describe what you found and one change you could make.
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response describes a specific multimedia element from the participant's actual courses. It shows that they checked for captions, applied the AD decision tree to a real example, and attempted keyboard navigation (not hypothetical reasoning). It identifies a specific accessibility gap and a concrete next step.
